---
title: "Get Started"
toc: true
---

This workshop runs entirely in the cloud using **GitHub Codespaces**. There is
nothing to install on your laptop — you only need a web browser and a free
GitHub account.

Follow the steps below to get a working environment.

## 1. Create a codespace from the participant environment

1. Open the [participant environment repo](https://github.com/elsherbini/data-science-for-biology-june2026-codespace) on GitHub.
2. Click the green **"Use this template"** button near the top right.
3. Choose **"Create a new repository"** and give it a name (for example,
   `workshop-2026`). This makes your own copy of the environment.
4. On your new repository, click the green **"Code"** button.
5. Open the **"Codespaces"** tab and click **"Create codespace on main"**.

GitHub will build the environment for you. The first build takes a few
minutes — after that, reopening the codespace is fast.

## 2. Set the `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` Codespaces secret

The workshop uses AI models through OpenRouter. You need to store your
OpenRouter API key as a Codespaces secret so the environment can use it.

1. Go to your GitHub **Settings** → **Codespaces**.
2. Under **"Codespaces secrets"**, click **"New secret"**.
3. Name the secret exactly `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`.
4. Paste in the API key you were given for the workshop.
5. Under **"Repository access"**, select the repository you created in
   step 1 so the secret is available to your codespace.
6. Save the secret. If your codespace is already open, rebuild or restart it
   so it picks up the new secret.

## 3. Open a notebook

Once the codespace has finished building:

1. Use the file explorer on the left to find the starter Quarto notebooks
   (files ending in `.qmd`).
2. Open one to start working. You can edit text and code cells directly in
   the browser.

## 4. Meet Claude Code in the terminal

Your codespace includes **Claude Code**, an AI coding assistant, in the
integrated terminal.

1. Open the integrated terminal (**Terminal → New Terminal**, or use the
   keyboard shortcut).
2. Start Claude Code by typing `claude` and pressing Enter.
3. Ask it questions in plain language — it can read your notebook, explain
   code, and help you make changes.

You are now ready to begin the workshop. If you get stuck, ask an instructor
or a teaching assistant.
